task: Parse an AWS CloudFormation template in JSON format and extract all resource logical IDs
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a single string parameter annotated with type hints (`template_str: str`) and return `list[str]`
- The function must raise `TypeError` with a descriptive message if the input is `None` or not a `str`
- The function must catch `json.JSONDecodeError` when parsing malformed JSON and raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the parsed JSON root is a `dict`; if it is any other type (e.g., list, string, number), raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the parsed dict contains a top-level `Resources` key; if the key is absent, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the value of `Resources` is a `dict`; if it is any other type, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the `Resources` dict is non-empty; if it is an empty dict `{}`, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message — returning an empty list for an empty `Resources` dict is explicitly incorrect
- The function must return a `list[str]` of all keys from the `Resources` dictionary as the logical IDs when all validations pass
- The function must include a docstring describing the expected input format, return value, and every exception that may be raised

task: Write a Python function to validate AWS S3 bucket names according to AWS naming rules
solution_criteria:
The function must accept a string and return True only if the name satisfies ALL of the following AWS S3 bucket naming rules, and False otherwise:
- Length must be between 3 and 63 characters (inclusive); enforce this exclusively within the regex pattern (do not add a separate len() guard — the regex alone is the authoritative length check)
- The main validation regex must be compiled once at module level as a named constant (e.g., BUCKET_PATTERN = re.compile(...)) and reused on each call — do not call re.compile() or re.match() with a raw string literal inside the function body
- Only lowercase letters (a-z), digits (0-9), and hyphens (-) are allowed; dots (.) are not permitted in this implementation
- Must start and end with a lowercase letter or digit (not a hyphen)
- Must not contain consecutive hyphens (e.g., 'my--bucket' is invalid); enforce this rule directly inside the main regex using a negative lookahead (e.g., (?!.*--)) rather than a separate 'if "--" in name' check outside the regex
- The main regex must therefore combine in a single pattern: the length bounds (3-63), the allowed character set, the start/end character constraints, and the consecutive-hyphen prohibition via negative lookahead
- Must not be formatted as an IPv4 address (i.e., reject strings matching the pattern of four dot-separated octets such as '192.168.1.1'); since dots are already excluded by the character set rule, explicitly document in the docstring that this rule is inherently satisfied, and additionally include a dedicated compiled IP-address regex constant at module level as a safeguard check
- Must include a docstring that lists each validation rule being enforced
- Must not use redundant or conflicting length checks (e.g., do not combine a regex length quantifier with a separate len() call)

task: Create a regex pattern to match valid AWS IAM role ARNs
solution_criteria:
- The regex must match all three AWS partitions: `arn:aws`, `arn:aws-us-gov`, and `arn:aws-cn`, using a named capture group called `partition` that matches only these three exact values
- The account ID must be captured in a named group called `account_id` and match exactly 12 decimal digits — no more, no fewer
- The role name must be captured in a named group called `role_name` and allow only alphanumeric characters (a-z, A-Z, 0-9), hyphens, underscores, and forward slashes — the character class must be explicitly written as `[a-zA-Z0-9_/\-]` (or equivalent) without using `\w` or including any additional characters such as `+=,.@`
- Each path segment in the role name (when split by `/`) must be non-empty, meaning consecutive forward slashes (e.g., `role//name`) must not match; enforce this by requiring each segment to contain at least one non-slash character, for example using a pattern like `[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+(/[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+)*`
- The `match_arn` function must accept a single argument and return `None` (without raising any exception) when the input is `None`, an empty string, a non-string type, or any string that does not match the ARN pattern
- All unit tests must be written using `pytest` or `unittest` and must not execute at import time; no test code or test function calls may appear at module level outside of a `if __name__ == '__main__':` guard or a dedicated test file
- Include at least five unit tests covering: a standard `arn:aws` ARN (assert correct `partition`, `account_id`, and `role_name` capture group values), a GovCloud `arn:aws-us-gov` ARN, a China `arn:aws-cn` ARN, an invalid ARN with wrong format (assert no match), and a `None` or empty string input (assert return value is `None`)
- Include one additional test asserting that a role name containing consecutive slashes (e.g., `role//name`) does not match the pattern

task: Write a Python function to convert AWS EC2 instance metadata JSON response into a flattened dictionary with dot-notation keys
solution_criteria:
- Accept input as either a pre-parsed dict or a raw JSON string, using json.loads() to parse string input before processing
- Return a flat dictionary where all nested keys are joined with dot-notation (e.g., 'network.interfaces.0.mac') and leaf values are preserved as-is
- Raise a TypeError with a descriptive message if input is None or not a dict or string type; this validation must occur unconditionally at every recursive call, not only at depth 0, so that any non-dict value reaching data.items() produces a clean TypeError rather than an AttributeError
- Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if a string input is not valid JSON
- Include a recursion depth guard that raises a RecursionError with a descriptive message when _depth reaches exactly 20 (i.e., use _depth >= 20 so that depth 20 is the first disallowed level, making 19 the true maximum allowed depth), and document this boundary precisely
- Include a complete docstring describing all parameters including the internal _depth parameter (its type, default value, and purpose), the return type, all raised exceptions, and an example using EC2 metadata structure
- Include type hints for all parameters and the return value, including _depth: int = 0 in the signature (e.g., def flatten_metadata(data: Union[dict, str], prefix: str = '', _depth: int = 0) -> dict)

task: Create a regex pattern to extract AWS access key IDs from logs or configuration files
solution_criteria:
- The regex must use a single capture group (parentheses) wrapping the entire 20-character key so the full key is returned via match group(1) or equivalent; no additional capture groups may split the key
- The pattern must match all four common AWS key ID prefixes — AKIA, ASIA, AROA, and AIDA — using a non-capturing alternation group (e.g., (?:AKIA|ASIA|AROA|AIDA))
- After the 4-character prefix, the pattern must match exactly 16 uppercase alphanumeric characters ([A-Z0-9]{16}), for a total captured length of exactly 20 characters
- The pattern must use a negative lookbehind (?<![A-Z0-9]) immediately before the prefix and a negative lookahead (?![A-Z0-9]) immediately after the 16-character suffix to prevent matching keys embedded within longer alphanumeric strings (e.g., 'XAKIA...' or 'AKIA...EXTRA' must not match); reliance on \b alone is insufficient and must not be used as the sole boundary assertion
- The pattern must be case-sensitive and match only uppercase characters; it must not use re.IGNORECASE or equivalent flags, and must not match lowercase variants of the prefix or suffix
- The solution must demonstrate the pattern correctly rejects strings where the key is preceded or followed by additional word characters (e.g., 'XAKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE' and 'AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLEXYZ' must both produce no match)

task: Write a Python function to parse AWS CloudWatch log timestamps and convert them to a human-readable format
solution_criteria:
- Accept a single parameter typed as `int` with a descriptive name (e.g., `timestamp_ms`), and include a complete docstring describing the parameter, return value, and any exceptions raised
- Validate the input type using a check that explicitly excludes booleans: use `if not isinstance(timestamp_ms, int) or isinstance(timestamp_ms, bool)` to raise `TypeError`, ensuring `True` and `False` are never accepted as valid timestamps
- Handle `None` explicitly before the general type check, raising `TypeError` with a dedicated descriptive message such as `"timestamp_ms must not be None"`
- Raise `ValueError` if the value is negative or exceeds a defined upper bound representing year 9999 in milliseconds (e.g., `253402300799999`); define this upper bound as a named constant inside the function body (e.g., `MAX_TIMESTAMP_MS = 253402300799999`) to keep it encapsulated and self-documenting
- Convert the milliseconds timestamp to seconds before constructing a UTC-aware `datetime` object using `datetime.fromtimestamp(ts_seconds, tz=timezone.utc)`
- Format the output using `strftime` with an explicit format string (e.g., `"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"`) to produce a consistent ISO 8601 string without microseconds or UTC offset suffixes
- Include type hints for both the parameter (`int`) and the return value (`str`)

task: Create a JSON schema that validates the structure of an AWS Lambda function configuration object
solution_criteria:
The JSON schema should define required fields (FunctionName, Runtime, Handler, Role) with correct data types, and must satisfy the following precise requirements:
- FunctionName must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9-_:]+$ and have maxLength of 140 to support both plain names (up to 64 chars) and full ARN format (up to 140 chars)
- Runtime must be an enum restricted to valid AWS Lambda runtime strings (e.g., python3.11, nodejs18.x, java17, etc.)
- Role must match an ARN pattern such as ^arn:aws:iam::[0-9]{12}:role/.+$
- Handler must match the pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9_/.-]+\.[a-zA-Z0-9_]+$ to enforce the AWS 'filename.method' format and have a maxLength of 128
- VpcConfig, when present, must require SubnetIds and SecurityGroupIds as arrays each with minItems of 1
- EphemeralStorage must be supported as an optional field with a nested Size property (integer, minimum 512, maximum 10240)
- Environment must contain a Variables object where: (1) all keys must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$ via patternProperties, (2) all values must be explicitly typed as strings in the patternProperties value schema, (3) additionalProperties must be set to false on the Variables object to reject non-matching keys, and (4) maxProperties of 128 must be enforced on the Variables object
- Timeout must be an integer with minimum 1 and maximum 900, and MemorySize must be an integer with minimum 128 and maximum 10240
- The top-level schema must set additionalProperties to false to prevent unknown or invalid Lambda configuration fields from passing validation silently

task: Write a Python function to filter AWS DynamoDB table items based on a simple attribute condition
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a list of DynamoDB item dictionaries in boto3 typed format (e.g., `{'attr': {'S': 'value'}}` or `{'attr': {'N': '123'}}`) and a condition specified as an attribute name and a plain Python value, returning only items where the typed attribute value matches the condition value after extracting the inner typed value
- The function must support at minimum the DynamoDB types `S` (string), `N` (numeric string compared using `decimal.Decimal` for both the stored value and the comparison value to avoid float precision issues and remain consistent with boto3/DynamoDB conventions), and `BOOL` (boolean)
- The function must explicitly document in the docstring that only the first key of a typed value dict is evaluated (consistent with DynamoDB's single-type-per-attribute format), and the implementation must reflect this intentional design rather than relying on implicit loop behavior
- The function must validate inputs and raise a `ValueError` if the items list is `None`, and silently skip or raise a `TypeError` for any non-dict entries in the list
- The function must include full type hints on all parameters and the return value
- The function must include a docstring describing parameters, return value, supported DynamoDB types (`S`, `N`, `BOOL`), explicitly unsupported types (`L`, `M`, `SS`, `NS`, `BS`, `NULL`), the single-key evaluation behavior, and any exceptions raised
- Unsupported DynamoDB type keys encountered during filtering must cause the item to be excluded from results (not raise an exception), and this behavior must be documented in the docstring

task: Create a regex pattern to match and extract AWS region codes from various AWS service endpoints
solution_criteria:
The regex must match standard AWS region codes (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-2, ap-southeast-1) as well as AWS GovCloud regions in three-segment format (e.g., us-gov-west-1, us-gov-east-1).
- The region pattern must explicitly model two distinct formats: a two-segment format ([a-z]+-[a-z]+-\d+) for standard regions and a three-segment format ([a-z]+-gov-[a-z]+-\d+) for GovCloud regions, rather than making '-gov' optional on a single pattern, to prevent partial or ambiguous matches.
- The left boundary of the region match must be explicitly anchored using (?:(?:^|\.)(?=...)) or a required literal dot (\.) immediately preceding the region group — not a negative lookbehind such as (?<![\w]) — so that region-like strings in non-endpoint contexts are not matched.
- The right boundary of the region match must require the region to be immediately followed by \.amazonaws\.com (with optional additional subdomains allowed after that suffix), ensuring the match is strictly bounded to valid AWS endpoint contexts.
- The regex must use a named capture group (?P<region>...) that isolates only the region code string, excluding any surrounding dots or URL components.
- The geographic prefix component must use [a-z]+ (one or more lowercase letters) rather than a fixed-length quantifier such as {2,3}, so that prefixes of any length (e.g., 'ap', 'eu', 'sa', 'us') are matched without hardcoding expected lengths.
- The directional or sub-region component (e.g., 'east', 'west', 'southeast', 'northeast') must also use [a-z]+ to accommodate multi-character directional tokens of any length.
- The solution must be implemented as a single compiled Python pattern (re.compile(...)) usable with re.search() or re.findall() against a full URL string, with no helper functions or multi-step parsing.
- The pattern must not produce false positives on dot-separated URL segments that resemble region codes but are not followed by .amazonaws.com (e.g., 's3', 'bucket', 'execute-api' must not be captured as regions).

task: Write a Python function to calculate AWS S3 storage costs based on object size and storage class
solution_criteria:
- Accept `size_bytes` (int/float) and `storage_class` (str) parameters, plus an optional `num_objects` (int, default 1) parameter; raise `ValueError` for negative `size_bytes`, non-positive `num_objects`, or unrecognized `storage_class` values
- Support storage classes: `STANDARD`, `STANDARD_IA`, `ONEZONE_IA`, `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, `GLACIER`, `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`
- Apply tiered pricing for `STANDARD` (first 50TB at $0.023/GB, next 450TB at $0.022/GB, over 500TB at $0.021/GB); all other classes use flat per-GB rates defined as named constants with inline comments noting values may become stale and should be verified at https://aws.amazon.com/s3/pricing/
- For `STANDARD_IA` and `ONEZONE_IA`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128KB; compute the monthly cost as `max(effective_size_gb, IA_MIN_SIZE_GB) * rate * max(actual_duration_fraction, IA_MIN_DAYS / DAYS_IN_MONTH)` where `actual_duration_fraction` defaults to 1.0 (one full month), `IA_MIN_DAYS = 30`, and `DAYS_IN_MONTH = 30.44` — this correctly bills for at least 30/30.44 of a month (≈0.986), not a full month
- For `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128KB and a 90-day minimum storage duration, computed as `max(effective_size_gb, GLACIER_IR_MIN_SIZE_GB) * rate * max(actual_duration_fraction, 90 / DAYS_IN_MONTH)`
- For `GLACIER`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 40KB plus 8KB overhead (48KB total) and a 90-day minimum storage duration; for `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, enforce the same 48KB minimum and a 180-day minimum storage duration; apply the same `max(actual_duration_fraction, min_days / DAYS_IN_MONTH)` pattern
- For `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, charge the storage rate at the frequent access tier ($0.023/GB) plus a monitoring and automation fee of `num_objects * ($0.0025 / 1000)` USD per month; clearly document in the docstring that the frequent access tier rate is assumed and that the infrequent access tier rate ($0.0125/GB) is not blended — callers needing blended rates must supply a weighted `size_bytes`
- Return the total monthly cost in USD as a float rounded to 10 decimal places
